## Detailed mathematical audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

The interpretation of irreducibility as “not a nontrivial join,” equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected, is reasonable and should be explicitly stated because the problem statement’s wording omits some standard qualifiers such as nonempty/proper subgraphs. This part is acceptable.

The statement that DKEP Lemma 8.5 specializes to finite factors with the trivial group action is essentially correct: DKEP Lemma 8.5 assumes a separable finite von Neumann algebra with an action whose restriction to the center is ergodic; for a factor and the trivial action, the center condition is automatic. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### 2. Normal support identification \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X\)

This is a **major gap**.

The proof uses the identification
\[
q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X,
\]
where \(e_X\) is the ordinary open support of the boundary piece \(X\subset B(L^2N)\), while \(q_X\) is the support of \((\mathbb K_X(N)^\sharp_J)^*\) in the DKEP normal bidual. DKEP does discuss \(p_{\rm nor}\), ordinary support projections of \(C^*\)-subalgebras, and the resulting normal-bidual corner inclusions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` needs to prove, not merely assert, that the support of \(X\) and the support of \(\mathbb K_X(N)\) agree after cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\).

The given argument is incomplete for several reasons:

- It does not precisely define \(A^\sharp_J\) or show why annihilation by all functionals in \(A^\sharp_J\) characterizes the normal support after cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\).
- It invokes “DKEP’s strong-closure proposition for normal \(N,JNJ\)-bimodular functionals” without a precise theorem number or a statement sufficient to imply the desired annihilator equality.
- It uses convergence in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\), but does not prove that every relevant \(\psi\in A^\sharp_J\) is continuous for the topology in which the closure is taken.
- It does not carefully handle passage from products \(S^*R\) to all of \(\mathbb K_X=(\mathbb K_X^L)^*\mathbb K_X^L\) and then to the normal-bidual support.

This support identification is foundational for all later \(q_U=p_{\rm nor}s_U\) computations. As written, it is not a complete proof.

### 3. The coefficient algebra \(\mathcal B_U\)

The paragraph defining \(\mathcal B_U\) contains several unproved assertions.

The author defines \(\mathcal B_U\) as the norm-closed linear span of coefficients coming from nonempty finite Connes fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\), \(Z\subset U\), and then says DKEP’s identities imply this is a \(C^*\)-algebra. DKEP does have the identities \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\) and \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But to conclude that the closed linear span is a \(C^*\)-algebra, one must check products of coefficients from possibly different fusions and show the resulting operators are again in the prescribed span. This is not fully written.

The statement that \(\mathcal B_U\) has the same ordinary support as \(X_{M_U}\) is also under-justified. The author uses the fact that \(P_L\in P_U B(L^2M)P_U\) when \(L\subset U\), but does not carefully prove that this implies \(X_{M_L}\subset X_{M_U}\) in DKEP’s boundary-piece sense. DKEP’s \(X_B\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by operators of the form \(xJyJe_B\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The inclusion is plausible, but the proof in `answer.tex` is only a sketch.

### 4. Lemma: intersection of graph-product boundary corners

This is the **central fatal gap**.

The lemma claims
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
The proof reduces this to the product-support inclusion
\[
\mathcal B_W\mathcal B_U\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_U.
\]

The key unsupported step is the sentence:

> Therefore the first factor on the right of (6) is supported by \(s_I\), while \(T_{\eta_2}\) is supported by \(s_U\), and the first inclusion in (5) follows.

This is not automatic. From knowing that a vector belongs to a correspondence whose coefficient boundary has support \(s_I\), one only immediately controls coefficient operators such as \(T_\alpha^*T_\beta\). To conclude that a **rectangular operator** \(T_\alpha^*\) lies in \(s_I A^{**}\), or that \(T_{\eta_2}\) lies in \(A^{**}s_U\), one needs a rectangular support lemma: for example, a proof that both \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) and \(T_\alpha T_\alpha^*\) are controlled by the relevant support, and that this implies the desired one-sided support relation. No such lemma is stated or proved.

This is not a minor omission: the entire support-sandwich argument depends on this rectangular support step.

The use of CDHJKN fusion rules is also too compressed. CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 give precise decompositions of \(M_{V_1}\)-\(M_{V_2}\) bimodules and their fusions. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) The author’s repeated-iteration use for arbitrary finite fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z\) and contragredients is plausible, but it is not written carefully enough to cover arbitrary bounded vectors in direct sums, off-diagonal coefficients, or infinite multiplicities.

Finally, the cut by \(p_{\rm nor}\) uses the assertion that all supports \(s_U\) commute with \(p_{\rm nor}\). DKEP gives such commutation for ordinary supports of appropriate \(C^*\)-subalgebras satisfying the normal multiplier hypotheses. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` does not verify those hypotheses for the newly defined \(\mathcal B_U\), especially simultaneous \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-bimodularity and faithful multiplier actions.

### 5. Finite palindromic boundary criterion

This lemma is one of the more convincing parts, but it still has some missing details.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}q_i^\perp\subset\widetilde S(N)\) is plausible because \(q_i\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\). The GNS argument with the palindromic product is also plausible.

However, the final hypertrace step relies on a u.c.p. map
\[
\Theta:B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 does produce natural u.c.p. maps from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual compact corner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) But `answer.tex` should explicitly state the exact DKEP construction being used and why it is \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular in the required sense. This is likely fixable, but as written it is still a cited black box with insufficient precision.

### 6. Relative Bass–Serre paradox proposition

The proposition is plausible but not rigorous enough as written.

The reduced-word commutator formulas
\[
[p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0
\]
are asserted by “direct inspection.” For a self-contained proof at this level, one must check them on all relevant reduced-word summands in the amalgamated-free-product \(L^2\)-decomposition, including the one-letter and \(B\)-summand cases. The proof also needs to explicitly show that these commutators lie in the relative compact boundary piece \(X_B\) or its \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure, so that DKEP Lemma 6.1 applies. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is about when commutator conditions on a dense generating set imply proper proximality of a point. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) `answer.tex` does not spell out the membership in the correct \(X_B\)-mixing/compact space.

The inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]
also need a careful \(B\)-valued inner-product proof. Orthogonality of the two images is asserted from \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\), but the proof should verify this on arbitrary \(Q\)-initial reduced-word vectors.

The “\(B\)-Haar” step for \(h=w_1a\) is plausible, but the proof should explicitly cover negative powers and show that the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal by computing \(E_B(h^{m-n})=0\).

These are not merely stylistic omissions: Proposition \(\ref{prop:BS}\) is the only source of relative proper proximality for the graph-product links, so it must be completely rigorous.

### 7. Application to graph products

The graph-theoretic part is mostly sound.

I cross-checked the elementary combinatorial implications by brute force for graphs with \(3\le |V|\le 6\): complement connectedness implies every vertex has a non-neighbor; in the singleton \(C(v)\) case there is an \(s\in\operatorname{Lk}(v)\) nonadjacent to the unique \(r\in C(v)\); the intersection of all links is empty; and connected complement with at least three vertices has no isolated complement vertex or isolated complement edge. This supports the author’s graph-combinatorial reductions.

The use of CDHJKN’s factoriality criterion is valid in spirit. CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 says factoriality fails only through universal vertices with nonfactor vertex algebra or through an exceptional nonedge pair with both dimensions \(2\). ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) In a connected complement graph with at least three vertices, those obstructions are indeed impossible.

The use of CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 for nonamenability is also valid in spirit: amenability would force every nonedge pair to be a two-dimensional exceptional pair adjacent to all other vertices, which is incompatible with connected complement on at least three vertices. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

However, because the earlier relative-proximality and boundary-intersection steps are not fully proved, the final application cannot be certified.

---

## Summary of blocking issues

The solution is **not answer-ready** because of the following essential unresolved gaps:

1. The identification \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X\) for arbitrary boundary pieces is not rigorously proved.
2. The construction and support equality for \(\mathcal B_U\) are under-justified.
3. The boundary-corner intersection lemma relies on an unproved rectangular-support principle.
4. The use of CDHJKN fusion rules in the support argument is not detailed enough to handle arbitrary bounded vectors/direct sums/off-diagonal coefficients.
5. The relative Bass–Serre proposition is an essential new lemma but is proved only by sketches of reduced-word and orthogonality arguments.
6. Therefore the palindromic criterion cannot be applied to the graph-product boundary pieces as claimed.

The theorem may well be true, and parts of the strategy look promising, but `answer.tex` does not yet constitute a complete rigorous proof.

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I use the standard graph-product meaning of irreducible: the finite graph
$\Gamma=(V,E)$ is not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement graph
$\Gamma^c$ is connected.  Traces are faithful normal tracial states.  For
$U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical trace-preserving expectation.

Let \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*
        =p_{\rm nor}A_N^{**}p_{\rm nor}
\]
be the normal bidual of Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson
\cite{DKEP}.  If \(X\) is an \(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\) is DKEP's
hereditary algebra associated with \(X\), and \(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  Thus
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=
 \{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\) and \(\widetilde S(N)\).  DKEP
Lemma 8.5 says that, for a separable finite factor, proper proximality relative
to \(X\) is equivalent to the non-existence of an \(N\)-central state on
\(\widetilde S_X(N)\) restricting to the trace.

\section*{Boundary support calculus}

We record the two pieces of support calculus used below.  First, if
\(B\subset A_N\) is a \(C^*\)-algebra which is an \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule
with faithful multiplier actions, and if \(s_B\in A_N^{**}\) is its ordinary
open support, DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives
\[
        (B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_BA_N^{**}s_Bp_{\rm nor},       \tag{1}
\]
and \(s_B\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\).  In particular \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}s\),
where \(s\) is the ordinary support of \(K_X(N)\).  DKEP Proposition 3.6
identifies \(K_X(N)^{\infty,1}\) with the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of
the original boundary piece \(X\), so this is precisely the normal-bidual
support used in Lemma 8.5.

Second, let \(B,C\subset A_N\) have ordinary supports \(r,s\).  If \(e\) is a
projection in \(A_N^{**}\) and
\[
        BC\subset eA_N^{**}s,\qquad CB\subset sA_N^{**}e ,
\]
then
\[
        r(sA_N^{**}s)r\subset eA_N^{**}e .                         \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, take approximate units \(b_i\to r\), \(c_j\to s\) weak-*, and pass to
successive weak-* limits in \(b_ic_jxc_{j'}b_{i'}\in eA_N^{**}e\).

We also use the following elementary rectangular version.  If \(D\subset A_N\)
has support \(d\) and \(T:L^2N\to H\) is bounded with \(T^*T\in D\), then
\(T=T d\) in the bidual sense: for an approximate unit \(u_i\) of \(D\),
\(Tu_i\to T\) strongly because
\(\|T(1-u_i)\xi\|^2=\langle (1-u_i)T^*T(1-u_i)\xi,\xi\rangle\to0\).
Consequently, if also \(S^*S\in C\) with support \(s\), then
\[
        T^*S\in dA_N^{**}s .                                      \tag{3}
\]

For \(U\subset V\), put \(\cH_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).  The vector
\(\hat1\otimes\hat1\) has coefficient projection \(P_U=e_{M_U}\), so DKEP
Example 3.4 realizes \(X_{M_U}\) as the hereditary algebra generated by the
\(M,JMJ\)-translates of this coefficient.  From the definitions
\(K_X^L=\overline{A_MX}^{\,\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}}\) and
\(K_X=(K_X^L)^*K_X^L\), and from
\(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\), \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), it follows that
\(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\) is the closed span of coefficients obtained from non-empty
finite fusions of \(\cH_U\) and \(\overline{\cH_U}\).  CDHJKN's fusion rule then
rewrites these fusions as direct sums of \(\cH_Z\)'s with \(Z\subset U\).
Accordingly let \(\cB_U\) be the norm-closed span of all coefficients
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), where \(\xi,\eta\) are bounded vectors in a non-empty finite
fusion of correspondences \(\cH_Z\) and \(\overline{\cH_Z}\) with \(Z\subset U\);
then \(\cB_U=K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).  It is a \(C^*\)-algebra since products and
adjoints are again coefficients:
\[
 (T_\xi^*T_\eta)(T_\alpha^*T_\beta)
   =T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{\,*}T_\beta,\qquad
 (T_\xi^*T_\eta)^*=T_\eta^*T_\xi .
\]
Let \(s_U\) be the ordinary support of \(\cB_U\).  By (1),
\[
        q_U:=q_{X_{M_U}}=p_{\rm nor}s_U .                          \tag{4}
\]

\begin{lemma}[intersection of graph-product boundary corners]\label{lem:intersection}
For all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
        q_W(q_U\cA_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\cA_Mq_{U\cap W}.     \tag{5}
\]
Consequently, if \(U_1,\ldots,U_m\subset V\) and
\[
R_m=q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m},
\]
then \(R_m\in q_I\cA_Mq_I\), where \(I=\cap_iU_i\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Put \(I=U\cap W\).  We first prove, in \(A_M^{**}\),
\[
        \cB_W\cB_U\subset s_IA_M^{**}s_U,\qquad
        \cB_U\cB_W\subset s_UA_M^{**}s_I .                         \tag{6}
\]
It suffices by norm closure to multiply two coefficient operators.  If the
first coefficient comes from a finite fusion using subsets of \(W\) and the
second from one using subsets of \(U\), then
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.    \tag{7}
\]
The CDHJKN bimodule decomposition and fusion rule \cite[Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN} say that every such finite fusion over subsets of
\(W\) decomposes as a direct sum of copies of \(\cH_L\) with \(L\subset W\),
and similarly for \(U\); after fusing the two \(W\)-parts with the \(U\)-part,
all summands containing
\(\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1\) are \(\cH_L\)'s with
\(L\subset I\).  Finite-support vectors in these direct sums are dense, and the
coefficient maps are norm continuous, so the assertion holds for arbitrary
bounded vectors.  Thus
\(T_{\alpha}^*T_{\alpha}\in\cB_I\) for
\(\alpha=\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1\), while
\(T_{\eta_2}^*T_{\eta_2}\in\cB_U\).  The rectangular support lemma (3) gives
\(T_\alpha^*T_{\eta_2}\in s_IA_M^{**}s_U\).  This proves the first inclusion
in (6), and the second follows by adjoints.

Apply (2) with \(B=\cB_W\), \(C=\cB_U\), \(e=s_I\).  We obtain
\[
        s_W(s_UA_M^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_IA_M^{**}s_I .             \tag{8}
\]
Since the \(s_\bullet\)'s commute with \(p_{\rm nor}\), cutting (8) by
\(p_{\rm nor}\) and using (4) gives (5).  The palindromic statement follows by
induction from
\(R_{k+1}=q_{U_{k+1}}R_kq_{U_{k+1}}\) and (5).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite palindromic criterion]\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_m\)
be boundary pieces such that, with \(q_i=q_{X_i}\),
\[
        R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{9}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to each \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume \(N\) is not properly proximal.  DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives an \(N\)-central
state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\) and
write \(q=q_i\).  Since \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\), for
\(T\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\) one has
\(q^\perp Tq^\perp\in\widetilde S(N)\).  If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), the
compression \(T\mapsto \omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is an
\(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  Its restriction to \(N\) is
normal and central; because \(N\) is a factor it is \(\tau\), contradicting
relative proper proximality.  Hence \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for every \(i\).

Extend \(\omega\) to a state \(\bar\omega\) on \(\cA_N\).  In the GNS space of
\(\bar\omega\), each \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, and therefore
\(\bar\omega(R)=1\).  Since \(0\le R\le q_0\), \(\bar\omega(q_0)=1\).  The
natural DKEP normal-bidual map
\[
        \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\longrightarrow q_0\cA_Nq_0
\]
is \(N\)-bimodular, u.c.p., and agrees with the identity on \(N\) after the
canonical embedding.  Thus \(\bar\omega\circ\Theta\) is an \(N\)-central state
on \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\) extending \(\tau\), i.e. a Connes hypertrace.  This
contradicts non-amenability of the finite factor \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations, and suppose
\(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).  Assume that \(A\) contains a trace-zero unitary \(a\) and
that \(P\) contains unitaries \(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Write \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\), \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\), and use the usual
amalgamated-free-product decomposition of \(L^2N\).  Let \(p\) and \(q\) be
the projections onto the sums of reduced tensors whose first letter is in
\(H_P\) and \(H_Q\), respectively; then \(1=e_B+p+q\).

Let \(R_x=Jx^*J\).  On reduced tensors, if \(x\in P\ominus B\), right
multiplication by \(x\) preserves the first letter except on \(L^2B\) and on
one-letter \(P\)-words.  Hence
\[
        [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0 .
\]
For \(x\in Q\ominus B\) the analogous identities hold with \(p\) and \(q\)
interchanged, and elements of \(B\) commute with both projections.  The two
commutators displayed lie in \(X_B\), since \(X_B\) contains the left and right
\(N\)-translates of \(e_B\).  DKEP Lemma 6.1 therefore gives
\(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) normal on \(N\).
Left multiplication by \(w_i\) sends \(qL^2N\) into \(pL^2N\).  The two images
are orthogonal: for reduced \(Q\)-initial vectors \(\xi,\eta\), the
\(B\)-valued inner product of \(w_1\xi\) and \(w_2\eta\) contains the centered
\(P\)-letter \(w_1^*w_2-E_B(w_1^*w_2)=w_1^*w_2\).  Thus
\(w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p\).  Similarly, since \(a\in Q\ominus B\),
\(apa^*\le q\).  Centrality gives
\(2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)\), so \(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

Finally \(h=w_1a\) is a \(B\)-Haar unitary.  For \(n>0\), \(h^n\) is the
reduced alternating word \(w_1aw_1a\cdots w_1a\); for \(n<0\) it is the
reduced alternating word \(a^*w_1^*\cdots a^*w_1^*\).  Hence \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for
\(n\ne0\).  Therefore the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise
orthogonal, because \(e_Bh^{k}e_B=E_B(h^k)e_B\).  They all have the same
\(\varphi\)-value, so \(\varphi(e_B)=0\).  This contradicts
\(\varphi(1)=\varphi(e_B+p+q)=1\).  By DKEP Theorem 6.2, \(N\) is properly
proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\).  The graph product splits as
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{10}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose trace-zero
unitaries \(u_r\in M_r\), \(u_t\in M_t\) and put \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  If
\(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and \(|V|\ge3\) give
\(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); put \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  In both
cases reduced-word freeness gives
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
The trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in the tensor
factor \(M_v\) of (10).  Proposition \ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly
proximal relative to \(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\in V\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
\(\cap_i L_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives
\[
 q_{L_{v_m}}\cdots q_{L_{v_1}}q_{L_{v_2}}\cdots q_{L_{v_m}}
 \in q_{\emptyset}\cA_Mq_{\emptyset}.
\]
Here \(M_\emptyset=\C\), and \(X_{M_\emptyset}\) is the compact boundary piece,
because \(e_{M_\emptyset}\) is rank one and its left and right \(M\)-translates
generate the finite-rank operators.

It remains only to check the factor and non-amenability hypotheses of Lemma
\ref{lem:finite}.  The graph product is separable.  By CDHJKN Main Theorem
0.5, factoriality can fail only through a universal vertex, or through a
non-edge pair adjacent to all other vertices with both vertex algebras
two-dimensional.  These are respectively an isolated vertex or an isolated edge
in \(\Gamma^c\), impossible because \(\Gamma^c\) is connected and has at least
three vertices.  Thus \(M\) is a factor.  By CDHJKN Proposition 6.3, amenability
would force every non-edge pair to be such an isolated two-dimensional pair,
again impossible in a connected complement graph with at least three vertices.
Hence \(M\) is non-amenable.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite} applies, and \(M\) is
properly proximal.

\section*{Problem interpretation}

The graph is finite, simple, and has at least three vertices.  The intended
``irreducible'' hypothesis is join-irreducibility: there is no nonempty proper
partition $V=S\sqcup T$ such that every vertex of $S$ is adjacent to every
vertex of $T$.  Equivalently the complement graph $\Gamma^c$ is connected.
All vertex traces are faithful normal traces.  A trace-zero unitary in each
$M_v$ implies each vertex algebra is nontrivial and provides centered unitaries
for reduced-word and amalgamated-free-product arguments.

For $U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical expectation.  If $B\subset M$, $X_B$ is DKEP's
boundary piece generated by the Jones projection $e_B$.

\section*{DKEP normal-bidual notation}

Let
\[
  \mathcal A_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
be DKEP's normal bidual.  For a boundary piece $X$, let $q_X$ be the support of
the normal corner $(\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*$ inside $\mathcal A_N$.  The
projection $q_X$ commutes with $N$ and with $JNJ$, and
\[
\widetilde S_X(N)=
\{T\in\mathcal A_N:[T,a]\in q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X
\text{ for every }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and $\widetilde S(N)$.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 applies to the trivial group action when $N$ is a separable
finite factor: $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X$ iff there is no
$N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction to $N$ is the trace.

Important caution: a state on a von Neumann algebra which is $1$ on two
noncommuting projections need not be $1$ on their lattice meet, because the GNS
representation of a non-normal state need not preserve meets.  Thus the naive
criterion ``relative to finitely many $X_i$ plus
$\bigwedge_iq_{X_i}=q_0$'' is not justified unless commutation/normality is
available.  The replacement is to use an actual positive palindromic product of
the $q_i$'s which lies in the compact corner.

\section*{Palindromic finite-boundary criterion}

Let $N$ be a separable nonamenable finite factor and $X_1,\dots,X_m$ boundary
pieces.  Put $q_i=q_{X_i}$.  Suppose
\[
  R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
Then relative proper proximality with respect to every $X_i$ implies ordinary
proper proximality.

Proof details:
if $\omega$ is an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S(N)$ with $\omega|_N=\tau$,
then for each $i$,
\[
  q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q_i^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
because $q_i$ commutes with $JNJ$ and $[T,JNJ]\subset q_i\mathcal A_Nq_i$.
If $\omega(q_i^\perp)>0$, compression by $q_i^\perp$ gives an $N$-central state
on $\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$ normal on $N$ (normality is by domination by a scalar
multiple of $\tau$ on $N_+$), contradiction.  Hence the GNS cyclic vector for
$\omega$ is fixed by each $q_i$ and therefore by $R$.  Since $R$ is a positive
contraction in $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$, $1=\omega(R)\le \omega(q_0)$, so
$\omega(q_0)=1$.  Restricting $\omega$ to $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong
\mathbb B(L^2N)$ gives a Connes hypertrace, contradicting nonamenability.

\section*{Graph-product boundary corner intersections}

For graph products write $q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}$.  The needed replacement for a meet
formula is
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
This implies by induction that the palindromic product associated with
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ lies in the corner for $\cap_iU_i$.

Reasoning.  Let $\mathcal H_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)$ with cyclic vector
$\zeta_U=1\otimes 1$.  The coefficient of $\zeta_U$ is $e_{M_U}$ and translates
of this vector generate DKEP's $X_{M_U}$.  DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ shows that products of a $W$-coefficient, a
$U$-coefficient, and a $W$-coefficient are coefficients of
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M \mathcal H_U\otimes_M \mathcal H_W
\]
(up to hereditary closure; the cyclic coefficient is
$e_{M_W}e_{M_U}e_{M_W}$).  Thus the normal corner on the left is contained in
the coefficient support of this triple fusion.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5 decompose this triple fusion as a direct sum of
$L^2(M)\otimes_{M_Z}L^2(M)$ with $Z\subset U\cap W$.  Since $X_{M_Z}\subset
X_{M_{U\cap W}}$, the whole coefficient boundary is contained in
$X_{M_{U\cap W}}$.

This is intentionally weaker than the unsupported identity
$q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}$ and is tailored to non-normal states.

\section*{Relative Bass--Serre paradox}

For $N=P*_BQ$, $Q=B\bar\otimes A$, with $A$ containing a trace-zero unitary $a$
and with unitaries $w_1,w_2\in P$ satisfying
\[
  E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0,
\]
one proves $N$ properly proximal relative to $X_B$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be the projections onto the free-product Fock subspaces whose
first letter is in $L^2(P)\ominus L^2B$ and $L^2(Q)\ominus L^2B$, respectively.
Then $1=e_B+p+q$.

Commutator formulas with right multiplication $R_x=Jx^*J$:
for $x\in P\ominus B$,
\[
  [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0,
\]
and for $x\in Q\ominus B$,
\[
  [q,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [p,R_x]=0.
\]
Elements of $B$ commute with $p,q$.  These formulas are checked directly on
reduced words; only the $B$-summand and one-letter words from the same free
component can change first-letter type.  Since right translates of $e_B$ are in
the boundary ideal, DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.

If $\varphi$ is an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$, then
\[
  w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q.
\]
The first inequality uses centeredness of $w_i$ and orthogonality from
$E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$; the second uses $a\in Q\ominus B$.  Centrality gives
$2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, so $\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.
The unitary $h=w_1a$ is $B$-Haar: the subspaces $h^nL^2B$ are pairwise
orthogonal.  Hence centrality forces $\varphi(e_B)=0$, contradicting
$1=e_B+p+q$.

\section*{Graph-product application}

For each vertex $v$,
\[
  M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\Lk(v)}}(M_{\Lk(v)}\bar\otimes M_v).
\]
Let $C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim v\}$.  Complement connectedness gives
$C(v)\ne\emptyset$.  If $C(v)$ has two vertices $r,t$, use trace-zero unitaries
$u_r,u_t$ and set $w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t$.  If $C(v)=\{r\}$, complement
connectedness and $|V|\ge3$ give $s\in\Lk(v)$ with $r\not\sim s$; set
$w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r$.  In both cases expectations of $w_i$ and $w_1^*w_2$ onto
$M_{\Lk(v)}$ vanish.  The vertex unitary in $M_v$ gives $a$.  Therefore $M$ is
properly proximal relative to every $X_{M_{\Lk(v)}}$.

The intersection of all links is empty.  The palindromic product of the link
boundary supports is therefore in the compact corner because
$M_\emptyset=\mathbb C$ and $X_{M_\emptyset}$ is the compact boundary piece.

CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives factoriality.  In a connected complement graph
with at least three vertices, there is no universal vertex and no isolated
exceptional nonedge component.  CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 gives nonamenability:
if $M$ were amenable, applying the relative amenability criterion with
$V_1=V,V_2=\emptyset$ would force every complement edge to be an isolated
exceptional edge, impossible in a connected complement graph with at least
three vertices.



\section*{Normal support calculus for correspondence boundaries}

For a correspondence $\mathcal H$, write $X_{\mathcal H}$ for DKEP's
coefficient boundary generated by $T_\xi^*T_\eta$ for bounded vectors.
Let $q_{\mathcal H}$ be the support in the normal bidual of
$\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(M)$.  A useful way to view this support is via the
normal left ideal $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ generated by the coefficient
operators $T_\xi$: the right support of $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ is
$q_{\mathcal H}$, and $q_{\mathcal H}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal H}$ is the weak-*
closed hereditary corner generated by the coefficients.

For correspondences $\mathcal H,\mathcal K$ over $M$, DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ gives the normal support-sandwich inclusion
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
 \subset
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}\mathcal A
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}.
\]
Proof template: first verify on generators
\[
T_\xi^*(T_\eta^*T_{\eta'})T_{\xi'}
 =T_{\xi\otimes\eta}^*T_{\xi'\otimes\eta'}.
\]
Then use separate weak-* continuity and approximate the right support
$q_{\mathcal H}$ by an approximate unit for
$\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}^*\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$.  This avoids assuming
commutation of supports or identifying their lattice meet.

Direct sums: the coefficient boundary of $\bigoplus_i\mathcal H_i$ is supported
by the join of the supports of the $X_{\mathcal H_i}$.  Diagonal coefficients
are immediate; off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the usual
$2\times2$ positivity/Douglas factorization argument.  Hence if every summand's
boundary is contained in $X_Y$, then the whole direct-sum boundary is contained
in $X_Y$.

For graph products, with $\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M$, CDHJKN fusion
gives
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_U
  \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset U\cap W}
       (L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M)^{\oplus k_Z}.
\]
Since $Z\subset I$ implies $e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_I}$ and $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_I}$,
the support of the fusion is dominated by $q_I$.  Combining with the
support-sandwich inclusion yields
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A q_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A q_{U\cap W}.
\]

\section*{Compact-corner hypertrace step}

In the finite-boundary criterion, do not identify
$q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$ with $\mathbb B(L^2N)$.  DKEP's proof of Lemma 8.5 gives
the needed replacement: a natural $N$- and $JNJ$-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
  \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0
\]
sending $1$ to $q_0$ and $x\in N$ to $q_0xq_0$.  If a central state
$\omega$ on $\widetilde S(N)$ satisfies $\omega(q_0)=1$, then
$\omega\circ\Theta$ is an $N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending the
trace.  This is a Connes hypertrace, forcing amenability of the finite factor.



\section*{Round 6 support-calculus refinement}

The critic objected that the previous support-sandwich argument multiplied
concrete operators after embedding them in the DKEP normal bidual, where the
canonical embedding is only u.c.p. and not multiplicative.  The safer route is:

Let \(A=B(L^2N)\) and \(p=p_{\rm nor}\in A^{**}\) be the DKEP projection for
simultaneous normality over \(N\) and \(JNJ\).  For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\),
write \(e_X\in A^{**}\) for the ordinary support projection of
\(\mathbb K_X(N)\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 implies \(e_Xp=pe_X\), and the
support projection in \(\mathcal A_N=pA^{**}p\) is \(q_X=pe_X\).  Thus one can
do support computations in the ordinary enveloping von Neumann algebra
\(A^{**}\), where the canonical embedding is multiplicative, and only then cut
by \(p\).

Concrete lemma: if \(B,C,D\subset A\) are hereditary \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule
\(C^*\)-subalgebras and \(CBC\subset D\), then
\[
e_C(e_BA^{**}e_B)e_C\subset e_DA^{**}e_D.
\]
Proof: approximate an element of \(e_BA^{**}e_B\) weak-* by a bounded net from
\(B\), sandwich by an approximate unit of \(C\), and pass to weak-* limits.
Cutting by \(p\), using \(e_\bullet p=pe_\bullet\), gives
\[
q_C(q_B\mathcal A_Nq_B)q_C\subset q_D\mathcal A_Nq_D.
\]
This addresses the nonmultiplicativity warning: no claim is made that
\(\iota_{\rm nor}(xy)=\iota_{\rm nor}(x)\iota_{\rm nor}(y)\).

For coefficient boundary pieces, the relevant product is not the previously
claimed double fusion but a triple/coefficient product.  If \(\mathcal H\) and
\(\mathcal K\) are \(N\)-\(N\) correspondences, then for bounded vectors
\[
(T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})(T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
 =
T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^*
T_{\xi_4\otimes\bar\xi_3\otimes\eta_2},
\]
using \(T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha\) and
\(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\).  Hence the algebraic coefficient product
\(X_{\mathcal H}X_{\mathcal K}X_{\mathcal H}\) is contained in the coefficient
boundary of
\(\mathcal H\otimes_N\overline{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K\).
DKEP Proposition 5.9/Theorem 5.10 and separate continuity of the relative
compact topologies are the intended justification for passing from algebraic
coefficient spans to the \(\mathbb K_X\)'s.

For graph products,
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) is self-contragredient.  Therefore
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset
q_{\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_U}\mathcal A_M
q_{\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_W\otimes\mathcal H_U}.
\]
Applying the CDHJKN fusion rule twice decomposes this triple fusion into
summands \(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\) with \(Z\subset U\cap W\), so
the support is dominated by \(q_{U\cap W}\).  This gives the desired
palindromic-product inclusion without using non-normal lattice meets.


\section*{Refined normal-left-ideal support mechanism}

For an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(\mathcal H\), DKEP's coefficient boundary
\(X_{\mathcal H}\) is generated hereditarily by the operators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\) bounded.  The most economical support object is
not the concrete \(C^*\)-algebra product
\(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}
\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), but the weak-* closed normal left ideal
\(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) generated by the coefficient maps \(T_\xi\).  Its
right support in \(\mathcal A_N=(B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) is
\(q_{\mathcal H}\).  This follows because \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the hereditary
algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and DKEP Proposition 5.9 identifies the
corresponding relative compact operators with the operators whose positive
squares lie in the relevant \(\mathbb K_X\)-corners.

Composition of rectangular normal bimodules is separately weak-* continuous in
the DKEP normal-bidual setting and agrees on bounded vectors with the concrete
Connes-fusion formula
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
  \mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}
  \subset \mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
Since \(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H}\in
\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\), for any
\(x=q_{\mathcal K}a q_{\mathcal K}\) one obtains
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}xq_{\mathcal H}
 =(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})^*a(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})
 \in q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}\mathcal A_N
    q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the support-level sandwich needed for graph products and avoids the
unproved closed-\(\mathbb K_X\) product inclusion.

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), the induced correspondence
\(\mathcal H_B=L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\) has coefficient boundary \(X_B\).  The
cyclic vector \(\zeta=\hat1\otimes_B\hat1\) has coefficient operator \(e_B\),
and \(N\)-\(N\) translates of \(\zeta\) produce exactly the left/right
translates of \(e_B\) appearing in DKEP's definition of \(X_B\).

Direct sums of correspondences: if each summand \(\mathcal H_j\) has support
below a projection \(q\), then \(\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\) also has support below
\(q\).  Off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the standard hereditary
corner fact \(a^*a,b^*b\in D\Rightarrow a^*b\in D\), proved from \(2\times2\)
positivity/Douglas factorization.  Infinite direct sums follow by truncating
bounded vectors.

\section*{Operator-system point in the palindromic criterion}

DKEP's \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) is an operator system, not generally a
\(C^*\)-algebra.  When a state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) satisfies
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for several support projections \(q_i\), extend
\(\omega\) to a state \(\bar\omega\) on the ambient von Neumann algebra
\(\mathcal A_N\).  In the GNS representation of \(\bar\omega\), each
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector; hence the palindromic product
\((q_1\cdots q_m)^*(q_1\cdots q_m)\) has \(\omega\)-value one.  This justifies
the GNS step without pretending that \(\widetilde S(N)\) itself is a
\(C^*\)-algebra.

Compression normality: if \(q=q_X\) and \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), the compressed
functional \(T\mapsto \omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is normal on
\(N\) because \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and, on \(N_+\), it is dominated by
\(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\).



\section*{Ordinary-envelope support calculus after Round 8}

The normal-bidual map \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\to p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\)
should not be used multiplicatively.  The safe pattern is:
work in the ordinary enveloping algebra \(A^{**}\), prove a support inclusion
there, and only then multiply by \(p_{\rm nor}\), using DKEP Proposition 2.10
(commutation of \(p_{\rm nor}\) with supports of hereditary \(N,JNJ\)-bimodular
subalgebras).

For a hereditary subalgebra \(C\subset A\), write \(e_C\in A^{**}\) for its open
support.  If a concrete hereditary-product inclusion \(B C B\subset D\) is
available, then
\[
  e_B(e_C A^{**}e_C)e_B\subset e_D A^{**}e_D.
\]
Proof: approximate elements of \(e_C A^{**}e_C\) by bounded nets from \(C\),
sandwich by elements and then an approximate unit of \(B\), and pass to weak-*
limits.  Cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\) gives
\(q_B(q_C\mathcal A q_C)q_B\subset q_D\mathcal A q_D\).

For coefficient boundaries \(X_{\mathcal H}\), the desired support inclusion is
best viewed as a rectangular-support statement rather than as a product of
weak-* closed left ideals in the normal bidual.  In ordinary \(A^{**}\), the
support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the right support of the rectangular module
generated by the concrete maps \(T_\xi\).  The concrete identities
\[
  T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*,\qquad T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha
\]
give
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 (T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^*
 T_{\xi_4\otimes\bar\xi_3\otimes\eta_2}.
\]
DKEP Proposition 5.9 (polar decomposition for rectangular relative compact
operators) and Theorem 5.10 (mixing/relative compactness equivalences) are the
references for passing from coefficient generators to hereditary closures and
supports.  The resulting support estimate is
\[
  q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A_N q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
  \subset
  q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}
  \mathcal A_N
  q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the replacement for the invalid binary-fusion/normal-left-ideal
argument.

In the graph-product case
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) is self-contragredient and
\(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\).  CDHJKN Proposition 5.5 gives
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_W
  \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset W}\mathcal H_Z^{\oplus k_Z},
\]
and fusing each summand with \(\mathcal H_U\) gives only summands
\(\mathcal H_Y\) with \(Y\subset Z\cap U\subset W\cap U\).  Direct sums are
handled through DKEP Theorem 5.10: finite-support bounded vectors are cyclic for
the direct sum, so the support of a direct-sum correspondence is below the join
of the supports of its summands.  Hence
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
  \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]

A point for further audit is the exact formulation of the DKEP rectangular
support statement: one should avoid claiming that \(\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}
\K_{X_{\mathcal K}}\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\subset
\K_{X_{\mathcal H\otimes\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes\mathcal K}}\) for arbitrary
correspondences, since it is too strong when \(\mathcal H\) is the identity
correspondence.  The correct assertion is the support-corner inclusion above.



\section*{Refined graph-product support route}

A safer way to prove the graph-product boundary intersection is to avoid an
abstract support-sandwich theorem for all correspondences and work with the
concrete coefficient algebras for the graph-product basic correspondences.

First, for a DKEP boundary piece \(X\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), let
\(e_X\in A^{**}\) be the ordinary open support.  The normal support used in
DKEP for \((\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) agrees, after cutting by
\(p_{\rm nor}\), with the ordinary support of \(X\):
\[
        q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X .
\]
Reason: \(X\subset \mathbb K_X(N)\) gives \(p_{\rm nor}e_X\le q_X\).  Conversely,
if a functional in \(A^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X\), DKEP's strong-closure
description of \(\mathbb K_X\) (the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)/strong
\(N,JNJ\)-bimodule closure in Section 3) forces it to vanish on
\(\mathbb K_X\).  Thus \(X\) and \(\mathbb K_X\) have the same annihilator among
normal-bimodular functionals, hence the same normal support.  This is the bridge
between the concrete coefficient boundary and the \(q_X\) appearing in
\(\widetilde S_X\).

For \(U\subset V\), set
\[
        \mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M .
\]
The vector \(\hat1\otimes_{M_U}\hat1\) has \(T_\zeta=P_U\), the Jones projection.
Its \(M\)-\(M\) translates generate \(X_{M_U}\) (DKEP Example 3.4/5.11).  Let
\(B_U\) be the norm-closed span of coefficient operators coming from non-empty finite
Connes fusions of the correspondences \(\mathcal H_Z\) and
\(\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\) with \(Z\subset U\).  The DKEP identities for
\(T\)-operators make this a \(C^*\)-algebra, and CDHJKN fusion decomposes all
such non-empty finite fusions into \(\mathcal H_L\)'s with \(L\subset U\).  Since
\(L\subset U\) implies \(X_{M_L}\subset X_{M_U}\) (because \(P_L\in P_U
\mathbb B(L^2M)P_U\)), this algebra has the same support as \(X_{M_U}\).  Let \(s_U\)
be this support in \(A^{**}\).  Then
\[
        q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}=p_{\rm nor}s_U .
\]

The elementary \(C^*\)-support lemma needed is:

If \(B,C\subset A\) are \(C^*\)-subalgebras with supports \(r,s\), and if a
projection \(p\in A^{**}\) satisfies
\[
        BC\subset pA^{**}s,\qquad CB\subset sA^{**}p,
\]
then
\[
        r(sA^{**}s)r\subset pA^{**}p.
\]
Proof: let approximate units of \(B\) and \(C\) converge weak-* to \(r\) and

## Literature validation

DKEP’s paper does contain the basic normal-bidual framework, boundary pieces, the hereditary algebra \(K_X\), the \(K_X^{\infty,1}\)-closure result, the original and normal-bidual proper proximality criteria, and the natural maps used in Lemma 8.5. In particular, DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_B\) using the norm-closed span of \(x_1Jy_1JTJy_2Jx_2\) with \(T\in e_BB(L^2M)e_B\), Proposition 3.6 identifies \(K_X^{\infty,1}\) with the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of both \(X\) and \(K_X\), and Lemma 8.5 gives the normal-bidual criterion for proper proximality under the ergodic-center hypothesis. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

However, the revised proof attributes to DKEP Proposition 2.10 a concrete support formula
\[
(B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_BA^{**}s_Bp_{\rm nor}.
\]
The visible statement of DKEP Proposition 2.10 gives normal-bidual inclusions/intersections for \(C^*\)-algebras with faithful multiplier actions, but it does **not** explicitly state this ordinary-open-support cutdown formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This is a substantive point because the later proof repeatedly identifies \(q_U\) with \(p_{\rm nor}s_U\).

CDHJKN does provide the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules in the needed general direction, and its Main Theorem 0.5 and Proposition 6.3 support the final factoriality and nonamenability checks. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) But the use of the fusion rules inside the boundary-support calculation still requires additional operator-norm and bounded-vector arguments not supplied in `answer.tex`.

## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem statement and interpretation

This part is acceptable. The author explicitly interprets irreducibility as join-irreducibility, equivalently connected complement graph. This is the standard graph-product interpretation and resolves the ambiguity in the problem statement.

### 2. DKEP normal-bidual setup

The general definitions are broadly consistent with DKEP. The use of Lemma 8.5 for a separable finite factor with trivial action is acceptable, since the center is trivial and hence the ergodic-center hypothesis is automatic. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

But the support notation is still too compressed. The proof needs a precise justification of the passage from ordinary open support projections in \(B(L^2N)^{**}\) to the support projections \(q_X\) in DKEP’s normal bidual. This is not merely notational; it is used crucially in Lemma \(\ref{lem:intersection}\).

### 3. Boundary support calculus

The new support lemma (2) is mathematically reasonable: if \(BC\subset eA^{**}s\) and \(CB\subset sA^{**}e\), the approximate-unit argument does give
\[
r(sA^{**}s)r\subset eA^{**}e.
\]
The added rectangular lemma is also basically correct: if \(T^*T\in D\), then \(T=Td\) for the support \(d\) of \(D\).

However, the first support assertion remains problematic. The paper states:

\[
(B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_BA_N^{**}s_Bp_{\rm nor}.
\tag{1}
\]

This is not proved in `answer.tex`, and the cited DKEP Proposition 2.10 does not visibly state this formula. It may be derivable with extra work, but that derivation is essential and absent. Without it, the equality
\[
q_U=p_{\rm nor}s_U
\]
is not justified.

### 4. The definition and identification of \(\mathcal B_U\)

This is still a major gap. The proof claims:

\[
\mathcal B_U=K_{X_{M_U}}(M).
\]

This is an essential lemma, but it is not proved. The argument says it follows from the definitions, the \(T\)-operator identities, and CDHJKN fusion. That is not enough.

Specific problems:

- DKEP Example 3.4 defines \(X_{M_U}\) using all operators in the corner \(e_{M_U}B(L^2M)e_{M_U}\), not only rank-one-type coefficient operators. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) One must prove that the finite-fusion coefficient algebra described by the author really recovers the DKEP hereditary algebra \(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).
- DKEP Example 5.11 gives a coefficient-boundary construction for a correspondence, but it does not automatically identify \(K_X\) for \(X=X_{M_U}\) with the norm-closed algebra generated by all finite Connes fusions of \(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- The proof does not handle off-diagonal coefficients arising from direct-sum decompositions carefully.
- The proof does not justify that coefficients of arbitrary bounded vectors in possibly infinite direct sums are norm limits of coefficients of finite-support vectors.

The last point is particularly serious. The sentence

> “Finite-support vectors in these direct sums are dense, and the coefficient maps are norm continuous”

is not valid in the form needed. Finite-support vectors are dense in Hilbert norm, but \(T_\xi\) is controlled by the bounded-vector/right-bounded norm, not merely by Hilbert norm. In infinite direct sums, truncations of a bounded vector need not converge in the operator norm of the associated \(T_\xi\). Thus the passage from finite-support vectors to arbitrary bounded vectors is not justified.

### 5. Intersection lemma

The revision addresses part of my previous objection by adding the rectangular support lemma. That helps with the formal step
\[
T_\alpha^*T_{\eta_2}\in s_IA^{**}s_U
\]
provided one already knows
\[
T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\in\mathcal B_I,\qquad T_{\eta_2}^*T_{\eta_2}\in\mathcal B_U.
\]

But the proof of \(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\in\mathcal B_I\) still depends on the unresolved direct-sum/bounded-vector issue above. CDHJKN gives decomposition and fusion rules at the bimodule level, but the proof must still show that the corresponding coefficient supports behave correctly under arbitrary bounded vectors and infinite multiplicity direct sums. The current draft does not.

Therefore Lemma \(\ref{lem:intersection}\), the central boundary-corner inclusion
\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W},
\]
remains unproved.

### 6. Finite palindromic criterion

This part is substantially improved and is likely correct modulo standard DKEP facts.

The compression argument using \(q_i^\perp\) is plausible. The GNS argument with the palindromic product is also sound. The final hypertrace step is supported by DKEP’s Lemma 8.5 proof, where a natural map from \(B(L^2M)\) into the normal-bidual \(K_X\)-corner is used. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Still, the proof should explicitly justify normality of the compressed restriction to \(N\), for example by domination by a scalar multiple of \(\tau\). This is fixable and not the main obstruction.

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre proposition

The revised argument is much clearer than before. The reduced-word commutator formulas, the inequalities
\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q,
\]
and the \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) are now plausible.

However, the proof is still somewhat compressed. In particular, the claim that DKEP Lemma 6.1 directly gives \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\) requires a precise identification of the relevant \(X_B\)-mixing space with the commutator target. DKEP Lemma 6.1 is stated for dual normal \(M\)-bimodules and \(X\)-mixing/proximal points, not directly as the simple criterion used here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This is probably repairable, but as written it is still a black-box application.

That said, the Bass–Serre proposition is less problematic than the boundary-corner intersection lemma.

### 8. Application to graph products

The graph-theoretic part is sound. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from complement-connectedness is correct. The AFP splitting over the link is standard.

The final factor and nonamenability checks are also essentially correct. CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives the relevant factoriality obstruction, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability characterization; connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices rules out the exceptional isolated complement vertex/edge cases. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But because the preceding relative-proximality and boundary-intersection machinery is not rigorously established, the final conclusion does not yet follow.
